questions.question: A language L is decidable if and only if:
options: Some TM accepts every string in L and may loop on strings outside L
Some TM halts on every input and accepts exactly the strings in L
No TM can accept any string in L
Every TM halts on every input
questions.explanation: Decidable (recursive) means there exists a TM that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input.

questions.question: Which property makes the Halting Problem undecidable?
options: Turing machines have infinite memory
Any candidate decider can be used to construct a contradictory input via diagonalization
Python is too slow to simulate all TMs
There are uncountably many Turing machines
questions.explanation: The proof builds machine D that uses the hypothetical decider H's own output to produce a contradiction, showing H cannot exist.

questions.question: HALT is semi-decidable but not decidable. This means:
options: We can prove halting instances but cannot detect non-halting instances in general
We can detect both halting and non-halting in finite time
We can never detect any halting instance
HALT is neither recognizable nor co-recognizable
questions.explanation: A semi-decider for HALT simulates M on w and accepts if M halts. It loops on non-halting pairs, so it can confirm halting but not non-halting.

questions.question: If both L and its complement L̄ are semi-decidable, then L is:
options: Undecidable
Decidable
Not even semi-decidable
Context-free
questions.explanation: Run the recognizer for L and the recognizer for L̄ in parallel. Exactly one will accept, giving a total decider that always halts.

questions.question: What does it mean for a language to be decidable?
options: It can be described by a regular expression
There exists a Turing machine that always halts and correctly accepts/rejects every input string
It has a finite number of strings
It can be parsed by a pushdown automaton
questions.explanation: A decidable (recursive) language has a Turing machine that always halts on every input: accepting strings in the language and rejecting strings not in it. This is stronger than recognizable (recursively enumerable), where the TM may loop forever on non-members.
